title: Can you trademark a sound? The landmark ruling in Shield Mark BV v Joost Kist EU Trade Mark Law | 27 November 2003 | Case C-283/01
excerpt: A cockerel, a melody, and a fight over registration Shield Mark BV, a Dutch marketing and PR company, sought to register a series of marks in the Netherlands that were neither words nor logos - they w‚Ä¶
body:
A cockerel, a melody, and a fight over registration Shield Mark BV, a Dutch marketing and PR company, sought to register a series of marks in the Netherlands that were neither words nor logos - they were sounds. Among them: the first nine notes of Beethoven's Fur Elise, and the crowing of a cockerel. Mr Joost Kist, trading under the name Memex, subsequently began using similar sounds commercially. Shield Mark sued for infringement, and the dispute eventually reached the Court of Justice of the EU on a reference from the Dutch courts. The central question was deceptively simple: can a sound constitute a registrable trade mark under the Trade Marks Directive (89/104/EEC), and if so, how must it be represented in an application?

The graphical representation requirement Under Article 2 of the Directive, a sign must be capable of being represented graphically in order to qualify as a trade mark. This requirement exists to serve the register's public function - third parties must be able to consult it and understand exactly what is protected. The graphical representation must be clear, precise, self-contained, easily accessible, intelligible, durable and objective. These requirements - drawn from the earlier Sieckmann decision on olfactory marks - formed the analytical framework the Court applied to each of Shield Mark's proposed representations.

Which representations passed - and which failed? The Court assessed several methods Shield Mark had used to represent its sound marks in the application, reaching different conclusions on each. Onomatopoeia - writing "Kukelekuuuuu" to represent a cockerel's crow - was rejected. Onomatopoeia varies between languages and individuals; it is inherently imprecise and subjective. Verbal description - describing the sound in words such as "the first nine notes of Fur Elise" - also failed. Such a description requires further interpretation and does not itself constitute the sound. A sound file or recording was not, in 2003, considered an acceptable graphical representation. A recording changes over time and is not itself a visual image capable of being placed on the register. Musical notation on a stave, however, passed. A conventional music score set out with notes, tempo and key satisfies the Sieckmann criteria. It is precise, self-contained, durable and - crucially - intelligible to anyone with musical literacy.

How intelligible must a representation be? One of the most practically significant aspects of the judgment concerns the degree of intelligibility required. The Court held at paragraph 63 that intelligibility does not demand immediate intelligibility - it is enough that it be easy. A musical score requires some musical knowledge to decipher, but that level of facility is sufficiently widespread that the stave remains an acceptable medium. This nuance matters greatly. A strict "immediate intelligibility" standard would have excluded almost all non-conventional marks, because any representation of a smell, colour, or sound will require some baseline of knowledge to interpret. The Court's more permissive standard preserved a workable space for such marks.

From sound marks to colour: the Mini Babybel question The principles in Shield Mark have cast a long shadow over subsequent disputes involving non-conventional marks. Nowhere is this clearer than in questions about colour marks - specifically, the adequacy of representing a single colour as a trade mark. Closer to home, a dispute before the English High Court brought the issue into sharp relief in the context of the appearance of Mini Babybel cheese: whether the specific shade of red used on its distinctive wax coating could be adequately represented and protected as a trade mark. The Shield Mark framework - and in particular its insistence on precision and self-containment - informed how courts approached the question of whether a colour swatch, a Pantone reference, or a verbal description would suffice. The lesson from Shield Mark is consistent across mark types: the representation in the register must do the work of defining the monopoly. Courts will scrutinise whether the chosen method is truly precise and objective, or whether it leaves too much room for subjective interpretation.

What practitioners should remember ? Sound marks are registrable in principle, but only if graphically represented to the Sieckmann standard. A music stave with full notation is the established route for musical sounds. Animal sounds and other non-musical sounds remain difficult to represent adequately. The intelligibility threshold is "easy", not "immediate" - opening the door to representations that require modest specialist knowledge. Colour and other non-conventional mark applicants must apply the same framework rigorously, ensuring their chosen representation is precise and self-contained rather than merely descriptive.

title: Who Owns AI-Generated Work? Copyright Authorship Under the CDPA 1988
excerpt: The UK's Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988 contains a unique provision on computer-generated works √¢¬Ä¬î one that was ahead of its time but is now being tested by generative AI at scale. What does it mean for legal tech platforms like Lexdoo?
body:
The UK's Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988 is one of the few copyright statutes in the world to address computer-generated works expressly. Section 9(3) provides that the author of a computer-generated work is the person who makes the necessary arrangements for its creation. This provision, drafted long before modern generative AI, is now at the centre of one of the most contested questions in IP law.

What Section 9(3) Actually Says

The critical phrase is "makes the necessary arrangements." In the era of large language models and diffusion models, this raises a genuine question: does the person who writes a prompt, selects a model, or designs the system's parameters qualify? The Intellectual Property Office has acknowledged the uncertainty but has not yet issued definitive guidance, and there is no appellate authority directly on point.

The Thaler Challenge

The DABUS litigation √¢¬Ä¬î in which Dr Stephen Thaler sought patent protection for inventions generated by an AI system √¢¬Ä¬î raised adjacent questions in the UK Supreme Court, which ultimately held that an inventor must be a natural person under the Patents Act 1977. While the copyright question is distinct, the case signals judicial caution about extending IP rights to AI outputs without clear statutory authority.

The Lexdoo Question

For AI legal platforms, this uncertainty has direct commercial consequences. When Lexdoo's AI drafts a contract clause or summarises a judgment, the question of who √¢¬Ä¬î if anyone √¢¬Ä¬î holds copyright in that output affects licensing, liability, and the terms on which the platform can operate. These are questions I engage with not just academically but operationally, and they make a compelling case for legislative clarification by the UK Parliament.

title: From Application to Registration: Navigating Trade Mark Prosecution at UKIPO and EUIPO
excerpt: Filing a trade mark is deceptively straightforward. Prosecuting it through examination, opposition, and (sometimes) appeal is anything but. A practical guide to the prosecution process across UKIPO and EUIPO √¢¬Ä¬î including what can go wrong and how to address it.
body:
Trade mark prosecution √¢¬Ä¬î the process of taking an application from filing through to registration √¢¬Ä¬î involves more strategic decision-making than most applicants expect. Understanding each stage of the process, and the pitfalls at each, is essential for practitioners advising rights holders on portfolio development.

The Application: Getting the Basics Right

A trade mark application requires identification of the mark, the applicant, and the goods and services to be covered (specified by reference to the Nice Classification). The specification of goods and services is the most consequential drafting decision at the application stage. Too broad, and the registration becomes vulnerable to non-use cancellation after five years; too narrow, and the rights holder lacks the scope needed to exclude competitors. IP Translator and subsequent EUIPO practice have clarified that class headings cannot be relied upon to cover all goods in a class √¢¬Ä¬î each item must be specified with sufficient clarity.

Examination: Absolute Grounds

Both UKIPO and EUIPO examiners assess applications against absolute grounds for refusal under Section 3 of the Trade Marks Act 1994 (UK) and Article 7 EUTMR (EU). Common objections include descriptiveness, lack of distinctiveness, and deceptiveness. Where an objection is raised, the applicant may respond with evidence of acquired distinctiveness through use √¢¬Ä¬î a process that requires careful assembly of sales data, advertising evidence, and consumer survey materials. The evidential threshold is high, and the cost of meeting it should be factored into filing strategy.

Opposition: Relative Grounds

After publication, third parties have a window √¢¬Ä¬î three months at UKIPO, three months at EUIPO √¢¬Ä¬î to file an opposition based on earlier rights. Most oppositions are settled by way of a co-existence agreement or consent letter. Where they proceed to a contested hearing, the key issues are typically likelihood of confusion (assessed by reference to the average consumer and the similarity of marks and goods) and reputation √¢¬Ä¬î whether the earlier mark's reputation gives rise to a claim of unfair advantage or detriment to distinctive character.

The Strategic Layer

Post-Brexit, UK and EU trade marks are entirely separate rights requiring separate filing and prosecution strategies. For clients seeking protection across both territories, the practical question is whether to file national applications at UKIPO and EUIPO separately, or to use the Madrid Protocol to designate both via a single international application. Each route has different cost profiles, different prosecution timelines, and different opposition risk exposure. Getting this decision right at the outset can save significant cost down the line.

title: IP Licensing Fundamentals: What Every Practitioner Needs to Know
excerpt: A licence is not just a permission slip √¢¬Ä¬î it is a carefully constructed commercial instrument that determines who can use IP, on what terms, and at what price. From exclusivity to royalty structures to termination triggers, here is what to watch for.
body:
Intellectual property licensing is where legal doctrine meets commercial reality. A well-drafted licence can unlock enormous value from an IP portfolio; a poorly drafted one can create disputes, undermine rights, or leave significant value on the table. Understanding the fundamentals is essential for anyone advising on IP transactions.

Exclusive, Sole, and Non-Exclusive

The threshold question in any licensing negotiation is exclusivity. An exclusive licence means the licensor cannot grant further licences in the licensed territory or field √¢¬Ä¬î and cannot use the IP itself within that scope. A sole licence is exclusive save that the licensor retains its own right to use. A non-exclusive licence leaves the licensor free to license others. The distinction matters enormously for pricing, sub-licensing rights, and √¢¬Ä¬î critically under the Trade Marks Act 1994 and the CDPA 1988 √¢¬Ä¬î whether the licensee has standing to bring infringement proceedings without joining the rights holder.

Royalty Structures

Royalties can be structured as a percentage of net sales, a fixed fee per unit, a lump sum, or some combination. In practice, minimum annual royalties √¢¬Ä¬î guaranteed payments regardless of commercial performance √¢¬Ä¬î are often used to ensure that an exclusive licensee does not "park" the IP without exploiting it. Milestone payments are common in pharmaceutical and technology licensing, where the value of the IP is contingent on regulatory approval or product launch.

Field of Use and Territory

Careful definition of the licensed field of use is essential. An overly broad licence may inadvertently grant rights the licensor did not intend to convey; an overly narrow one may limit the licensee's ability to exploit the IP commercially. Territory must be defined with equal precision √¢¬Ä¬î particularly post-Brexit, where UK and EU rights are now separate instruments requiring separate licensing analysis.

Termination and Reversion

Termination provisions are often where licensing disputes originate. Practitioners should consider: what triggers termination for breach, what cure periods apply, what happens to sub-licences on termination, and whether IP developed by the licensee during the term reverts or remains with the licensee. In exclusive deals, the licensor's ability to recover rights and re-licence to a third party is often the most commercially sensitive point in negotiation.
